title: Why Hydration Matters More Than You Think

blocks.context: = measurable aerobic decline — 2-7% in controlled studies, worse in heat

blocks.content:
That threshold isn't theoretical. A 165-lb rider who loses just 3.3 lbs of fluid — roughly 53 oz — crosses the point where the body can no longer cool itself efficiently. Cardiac output drops. Blood thickens. Your muscles get less oxygen per heartbeat. In cool conditions the performance cost is small; in heat, it compounds fast.

I learned this the hard way at BWR 2019. I showed up with two bottles for a 130-mile race through the San Diego backcountry in June. By mile 60, I was rationing sips. My race-day hydration plan was "bring bottles and hope for the best." This is not a plan.

Gravel riders are uniquely vulnerable to dehydration for three reasons:

- **Duration**: Most gravel events run 4-12+ hours. That's 4-12+ hours of continuous fluid loss with limited resupply.
- **Remote terrain**: Unlike road races, there are no spectators handing up bottles every 20 minutes. You carry what you carry.
- **Limited aid stations**: Unbound 200 has just two full checkpoints — plus two water oases — across 200 miles of Kansas flint hills. BWR has long stretches with zero support. If you miscalculate your fluid needs, there's no safety net.

Road cyclists can afford to be sloppy with hydration. A domestique can grab a musette from the team car. A century rider can stop at a gas station. Gravel strips away those safety nets and punishes poor planning with DNFs.

blocks.question: At what percentage of body weight loss does measurable performance decline begin?

options.text: 2% — the threshold where aerobic performance measurably declines
5% — it takes significant loss to affect performance
It varies too much to give a number

blocks.explanation: Dehydration research consistently points to roughly 2% body weight loss as the inflection point where aerobic performance starts to measurably decline — the same threshold used in the ACSM's position stands. The size of the decline depends on conditions: a few percent in cool weather, considerably more in heat. Some studies suggest even 1-1.5% can impair performance in hot conditions, but 2% is the well-established benchmark.

blocks.content:
Here's what most hydration articles skip: **dehydration doesn't just affect your legs — it wrecks your brain.**

At 2-3% body weight loss, cognitive function measurably declines. Reaction time slows. Decision-making deteriorates. Your ability to assess risk — which line to take through a rocky descent, whether to push the pace or back off — gets worse precisely when the stakes are highest.

Controlled studies of dehydrated athletes show slower reaction times and degraded decision-making under fatigue. On gravel, where you're constantly reading terrain and choosing lines through loose rock, that cognitive impairment can be the difference between finishing and crashing.

This is especially dangerous in the back half of ultra-distance events. Hours 8-12 of Unbound aren't just physically demanding — they require sharp decision-making through fatigue, heat, and dwindling resources.

In this course, you'll learn to calculate your personal sweat rate, build an electrolyte strategy matched to your physiology, and create a race-day hydration plan that accounts for aid station spacing, temperature, and intensity. No generic advice — just science you can execute.

One rule governs everything that follows: **thirst is your floor, your measured sweat rate is your ceiling.** Never ignore thirst — adrenaline and racing suppress it, so a schedule keeps you honest. But never force fluid past your measured rate either; Lesson 4 covers why overdrinking can be more dangerous than underdrinking.

Before you open Lesson 2: put a sweat-rate test on your calendar. One ride this week, 60-90 minutes, with a nude weigh-in before and after. That single number turns the rest of this course from theory into your personal plan — and Lesson 2 gives you the exact protocol and the calculator to go with it.
